summary: Gruber uses Paul Thurrott's CES coverage as a springboard to examine the history of tablet computing, centering on Apple's Newton MessagePad as the true original tablet. He argues the Newton failed not because of any single flaw but due to a confluence of factors: it was too big, too expensive for what it offered, lacked wireless networking, shipped unreliable cursive recognition it shouldn't have included, and was positioned as a standalone system rather than a peripheral. He contrasts the Newton's trajectory with Palm's smarter tradeoffs and the iPhone's disciplined focus on practical concerns, suggesting that Apple circa 2010 has learned these lessons and is poised to get the tablet right.
key_points: The hype around Apple's rumored tablet isn't about inventing tablet computing — it's about making the first great one, just as Kubrick didn't invent sci-fi but made a masterpiece
The Newton failed due to multiple compounding factors: size, price, lack of networking, unreliable cursive recognition, poor syncing, and hubris in positioning it as standalone rather than peripheral
Palm succeeded where Newton failed by making smarter tradeoffs — smaller, cheaper, with a clear relationship to the user's computer
A hallmark of Jobs-era Apple is shipping only features that work great, even if that means leaving features out entirely
The Newton's biggest lesson is that being too big and too expensive isn't fatal in itself — being too big and too expensive for what you offer is
money_quotes: The hype isn't about Apple possibly unveiling the first tablet computing device; it's about Apple possibly unveiling the first great one.
I imagine Thurrott as a film critic in 1968, irritated at the hype surrounding Kubrick's 2001: A Space Odyssey on the grounds that Ed Wood made Plan 9 From Outer Space nine years earlier.
His gifts are for leading the creation of tech products from conception onward, not for taking products created by others and fixing them.
Nothing good comes of Apple products that Jobs doesn't like.
One of the hallmarks of Apple under Jobs is that they tend to ship only features that work great, even if that leaves many features not there at all.
There's a big difference between something that works well and something that's amazing only insofar as that it works at all.
I think the Newton would have had a better reputation for handwriting recognition if it had done less, by only accepting print notation.
The Newton wasn't too big, too expensive, period — I'd say it was too big and too expensive given what it offered.
key_insight: The Newton's failure and Palm's success demonstrate that getting the tradeoffs right matters more than ambition or technological achievement — a lesson that Jobs-era Apple internalized and would apply to the upcoming tablet.
